title: Site Intelligence Agent
description: Multi-agent RAG for field technicians working with messy, high-stakes operational data. Three routing paths: confident answer with citations, conflict surfaced for human review, programmatic escalation when the system can't trust its own output. Under 2% hallucination rate across 85 adversarial eval cases.
proves: Designs for failure, not the happy path
design_note: The core product decision was the routing threshold: at what confidence score does the agent stop and surface the conflict to a human instead of answering? Too low and you flood reviewers. Too high and you ship hallucinations. That threshold is a product call, not an engineering call.

title: Pipeline Risk Intelligence Agent
description: Spatial risk scoring with HITL conflict detection on real PHMSA pipeline data. Composite risk score layers FEMA flood zones, USGS seismic data, NLCD land cover, and historical inspection records. The HITL routing layer surfaces disagreement between spatial risk score and inspection history — gives the human reviewer a plain-English explanation and a counterfactual.
proves: HITL conflict detection is the differentiator, not the risk scoring
design_note: The key design decision was what the human reviewer actually sees when the spatial risk score and inspection history disagree. Showing the raw score disagreement isn't enough — the reviewer needs a plain-English explanation and a counterfactual to make a real decision. Designing that interface is product work.

title: MAGMA — Multi-Graph Agentic Memory
description: Interactive demo of a four-graph memory architecture (semantic, temporal, causal, entity) for agents reasoning across spatial and temporal histories — each graph answers a different query class. Built as a gamified 5-act walkthrough showing live graph traversal. Recognized by faculty as the standout project in the cohort.
proves: Translates research architecture into interactive proof of system thinking
design_note: The product question driving the build: how do you make a four-graph memory architecture legible to someone who didn't build it? The 5-act gamified structure was the answer — each act isolates one memory type so the system behavior is visible, not just described.

title: CallingServices MCP
description: Python MCP server for ticket intake and routing — three tools, one prompt, one resource over the Model Context Protocol. Claude Desktop picks it up with a single config line.
proves: Protocol-level systems thinking
design_note: Email header injection is blocked at input validation, not at send time. Security at the boundary means the bad input never reaches the system — not caught mid-flight.

title: Decision Intelligence Agent
description: Deterministic revenue classifier and marketing decision engine — deliberately no LLM in the core path. When revenue is healthy, it suppresses low-priority actions. Models the cost of acting.
proves: Knows when NOT to use AI
design_note: No LLM in the core decision path. The cost of acting is modeled explicitly — when revenue is healthy, suppressing low-priority actions is the right call, not an oversight.

title: Email Negotiation Coach
description: 3-step LLM chain that coaches negotiation — extract facts, diagnose gaps, generate questions. Deliberately does not draft replies. Human judgment stays in the loop by design, not by accident.
proves: Keeps humans in the loop by design, not by accident

title: Case Studies Dashboard
description: Live React + Gemini 2.5 Flash dashboard for MBA case analysis. Tab-aware context: system prompt rebuilds per active tab so 'what does this chart mean?' works without confusion.
proves: Context-aware prompting at the application layer

title: Ground Truth — Multi-Tab AI Dashboard
description: React + Anthropic API dashboard with per-tab system prompts — each tab rebuilds context from scratch so queries resolve correctly regardless of which tab is active.
proves: Context isolation at the application layer

title: DTI × ServiceNow — Complexity Cliff
description: Cornell DTI × ServiceNow consulting engagement. Core finding: the #1 drop-off cause isn't model quality — it's the 'complexity cliff,' the moment a low-code tool hits a scripting requirement with no bridge.
proves: Root-cause diagnosis in enterprise AI adoption

title: LaTeX Resume Tailor MCP
description: MCP server with 5 post-LLM validation checks — never adds experiences, never removes metrics, never changes LaTeX structure. Reliability engineering for AI outputs.
proves: Post-LLM reliability engineering

title: Agent PM — Daily Brief Synthesizer
description: Claude-based agent that pulls from Canvas and Microsoft To Do, synthesizes them into a daily execution brief. Shows multi-connector agent design and tool orchestration.
proves: Multi-connector agent orchestration

title: Agentic Consulting Analyst
description: Full system design docs, evaluation framework, and LinkedIn carousel for an agentic consulting analyst. Consulting-grade problem framing for enterprise AI pitches.
proves: Consulting-grade AI system design

title: 2D World AI — Learning Lab
description: Chapter-by-chapter lab where AI concepts — deterministic agents, random agents, LLM navigation, evals, LLM-as-judge — are built into a 2D grid world. Each chapter ends with a notes.md written after running the code. The notes are the artifact.
proves: Learning AI engineering in public, with evals

title: Waymo — Map Readiness Brief
description: Unsolicited PM brief arguing Waymo's construction-zone failures are a data provenance problem, not a perception problem — proposing a Pre-Event Map Invalidation layer. Built before applying.
proves: Forward-deployed PM: builds before applying

title: HomeLight — Geospatial Intelligence Brief
description: Hypothesis-driven PM brief on HomeLight's missing neighborhood intelligence layer — the conversion gap between agent matching and closing that Redfin/Zillow have closed. Built before applying.
proves: Forward-deployed PM: builds before applying

title: Netic — Technician Intelligence Brief
description: PM brief arguing Netic optimizes for job count but the real opportunity is job value — a technician intelligence layer that closes the data loop from the Cultivate Engine to the field and back.
proves: Forward-deployed PM: builds before applying

title: Drafted — Builder Retention Brief
description: PM brief for Drafted (YC P26) on the builder retention gap — 65% consumer users, 15% builders, but builders drive revenue. Maps the professional workflow gap the consumer-first product creates.
proves: Forward-deployed PM: builds before applying

title: Why I Design AI That Fails Gracefully
description: Most AI demos only show the happy path. This post explores why the routing threshold — knowing when NOT to answer and when to surface conflict to a human — is the hardest product decision in agentic system design. Not an engineering decision. A product one.
proves: Structured thinking on AI design tradeoffs

title: The PM Communication Arc
description: What my writing looked like 18 months ago vs. now — and the specific things I changed. A meta-post on how communication skill compounds.
proves: Learning in public about the craft of PM writing

title: Learning: Model Context Protocol
description: Deep dive into MCP — from reading the spec to building two servers. What the protocol actually solves (custom glue code per-client) and where it falls short.
proves: From spec to two shipped servers

title: Learning: RAG Architecture Patterns
description: From naive retrieval to confidence-gated pipelines. What similarity thresholds actually mean, how to tune them against a ground-truth eval set, and when to skip the LLM entirely.
proves: From theory to production tuning

title: Abhiroop Bhatnagar
author_title: VP, Cloud Solutions · Aereo · Direct Manager
description: No matter how complex or ambiguous the challenge, he approaches it calmly, breaks it down, and finds a clear path forward. I always had full confidence in him to execute with excellence and lead initiatives from start to finish.

title: Rajtosh Ranjan
description: He is one of the most focused and structured individuals I have ever met. He has a deep understanding of product requirements and knows how to guide and manage teams effectively to achieve the desired outcomes.
